title: Interpret Robot Task Execution Flow From WCS Through RMS To AGV
summary: Use the training-video-described control flow to explain how a robot movement task originates in WCS, is queued in RMS, and is executed by RMS through commands sent to the AGV.
when_to_use: Use when explaining or interpreting the high-level task execution path described in the training source for robot movement requests, including where tasks originate, where they queue, and how AGVs receive execution commands.
not_for: Do not use for UI navigation, queue-name lookup, or command-format details, because the source does not provide them.
Do not use to infer system interfaces, queue internals, or lower-level execution behavior not explicitly stated in the source.
Do not use as a recovery or control procedure for changing live system behavior.

roles_and_responsibilities.responsibility: Interpret and explain the source-described WCS-to-RMS-to-AGV task flow at a high level using the training content only.

safety_notes: This is an interpretation/reference procedure, not an operational control workflow.
Do not infer queue details, command formats, or system interfaces not stated in the source.
access_or_tools_needed: Description of the robot task or movement request
Source-backed WCS, RMS, and AGV task flow explanation

system_preconditions: A robot movement request or task scenario is available to interpret against the source-described flow.
external_references: UPSFORTNA OptiSweep Training Day 1, segment training_video_day1_segment_0006

visual_references.description: Training frame describing Geek+ RMS responsibilities, AGV control, movement handling, and route determination.

steps.title: Identify the movement task being discussed
steps.purpose: Establish the specific robot movement request or example that will be interpreted using the source-described flow.
steps.instruction: Identify the robot movement request or task being discussed, such as moving from one location to another.
steps.expected_result: A specific movement task or request is defined for explanation.
steps.healthy_condition: The task can be stated clearly as a movement request to be traced through the system.
steps.failure_condition: The task being discussed is unclear or cannot be framed as a movement request.

actions.label: Identify task scenario
actions.exact_action: Identify the robot movement request or task being discussed, such as moving from one location to another.

stop_or_escalate_if: The reported behavior or question cannot be tied to a specific movement request described at a high level.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript says the office software, described as WCS, assigns robot tasks that go into a queue in RMS, and RMS executes them one by one by sending commands to the AGV.

steps.title: Associate task origin with WCS
steps.purpose: Place the origin of the task in the correct source-described system component.
steps.instruction: Associate the task origin with the office software described in the source as WCS.
steps.expected_result: The task is understood to originate from WCS according to the source.
steps.healthy_condition: The explanation identifies WCS as the assigning office software.
steps.failure_condition: The task origin is attributed to another component without source support.

actions.label: Map origin to WCS
actions.exact_action: Associate the task origin with the office software described in the source as WCS.

stop_or_escalate_if: The behavior being analyzed cannot be explained by WCS assigning the task at a high level.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript says the office software, described as WCS, assigns robot tasks that go into a queue in RMS, and RMS executes them one by one by sending commands to the AGV.

source_evidence.retrieval_text: The WCS assigns robot movement tasks. Those tasks are queued in RMS, and RMS executes them sequentially by sending commands to the AGV.

steps.title: Note that tasks queue in RMS
steps.purpose: Identify where the source says assigned tasks are held before execution.
steps.instruction: Note that the source states the assigned tasks go into a queue that lives in RMS.
steps.expected_result: The task is understood to enter an RMS queue after assignment.
steps.healthy_condition: The explanation places the queue in RMS, consistent with the source.
steps.failure_condition: The queue is attributed elsewhere or queue behavior is expanded beyond what the source states.

actions.label: Place queue in RMS
actions.exact_action: Note that the source states the assigned tasks go into a queue that lives in RMS.

stop_or_escalate_if: Additional queue details are needed beyond the source's high-level statement.
Someone requests queue names, queue logic, or queue internals not provided by the source.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript says the office software, described as WCS, assigns robot tasks that go into a queue in RMS.

source_evidence.retrieval_text: The WCS assigns robot movement tasks. Those tasks are queued in RMS, and RMS executes them sequentially by sending commands to the AGV.

steps.title: Interpret RMS as the executor of queued tasks
steps.purpose: Explain the source-described execution role of RMS in relation to the AGV.
steps.instruction: Interpret RMS as the component that executes queued tasks one by one by sending commands to the AGV.
steps.expected_result: RMS is understood as the system that executes queued tasks sequentially through AGV commands.
steps.healthy_condition: The explanation states that RMS executes queued tasks one by one and sends commands to the AGV.
steps.failure_condition: Execution responsibility is incorrectly assigned directly to the AGV or another component without source support.

actions.label: Assign execution role to RMS
actions.exact_action: Interpret RMS as the component that executes queued tasks one by one by sending commands to the AGV.

screens_or_images.what_to_look_at: Look for statements that Geek+ RMS directly controls AGVs, handles movement, and determines routing from point A to point B.

stop_or_escalate_if: The reported behavior cannot be explained by RMS executing queued tasks sequentially.
A deeper interface or command-level explanation is required but not present in the source.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript says the office software, described as WCS, assigns robot tasks that go into a queue in RMS, and RMS executes them one by one by sending commands to the AGV.
Slide text indicates Geek+ AGVs are directly controlled by Geek+ RMS, Geek+ handles all movement, and determines the best route from point A to point B.

source_evidence.retrieval_text: Geek+ AGVs operate over QR codes and are directly controlled by Geek+ RMS. RMS handles all movement, determines the best route from point A to point B, and serves as the robot management system external to the AGV.

steps.title: Explain that the AGV receives commands rather than holding the higher-level task sequence
steps.purpose: Clarify the source-described division of responsibility between RMS and the AGV.
steps.instruction: Use this flow to explain that the AGV does not hold the higher-level task sequence knowledge described in the source; it receives commands from RMS.
steps.expected_result: The user can explain that higher-level task sequencing resides with RMS, while the AGV receives execution commands.
steps.healthy_condition: The explanation distinguishes AGV onboard sensing/vehicle functions from RMS task and movement control responsibilities.
steps.failure_condition: The AGV is described as independently owning the higher-level task sequence without source support.

actions.label: Explain AGV versus RMS responsibility
actions.exact_action: Use this flow to explain that the AGV does not hold the higher-level task sequence knowledge described in the source; it receives commands from RMS.

screens_or_images.what_to_look_at: Look for the slide and transcript content showing RMS as the robot management system that receives tasks, maintains the queue, determines routing, and sends commands to AGVs, while AGV hardware handles onboard sensing.

stop_or_escalate_if: A reported behavior cannot be explained by the source-described WCS-to-RMS-to-AGV flow because the segment provides only a high-level overview.
Someone asks for onboard AGV logic, command formats, or interfaces not stated in the source.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript says the office software, described as WCS, assigns robot tasks that go into a queue in RMS, and RMS executes them one by one by sending commands to the AGV.

The transcript explains the division of responsibility: onboard AGV hardware includes physical and collision sensors and reports obstacle conditions, while RMS acts as the robot management system, receives tasks, maintains the queue, determines routing, sends commands to AGVs, and generates alarm codes visible on the RMS screen.

source_evidence.retrieval_text: Geek+ AGVs operate over QR codes and are directly controlled by Geek+ RMS. RMS handles all movement, determines the best route from point A to point B, and serves as the robot management system external to the AGV.

success_criteria: The user can explain that WCS assigns the task.
The user can explain that the task is queued in RMS.
The user can explain that RMS executes queued tasks one by one by sending commands to the AGV.
The user can explain that the AGV receives commands rather than holding the higher-level task sequence described in the source.
healthy_conditions: The explanation remains consistent with the source's high-level WCS-to-RMS-to-AGV flow.
RMS is described as the robot management layer handling movement execution and route determination.
No unsupported queue, interface, or command details are added.
failure_conditions: The task origin, queue location, or execution role is attributed to the wrong component.
The AGV is described as owning higher-level task sequencing without source support.
Unsupported details are inferred about queue internals, command formats, or interfaces.
The reported behavior requires more than the high-level overview provided by the source.
escalation_guidance: Escalate if a reported behavior cannot be explained by the source-described WCS-to-RMS-to-AGV flow, because the segment provides only a high-level overview.
Escalate when lower-level queue behavior, command formats, or system interface details are required.
Escalate for SME review if the interpretation would affect troubleshooting or operational decisions beyond this source's scope.

screens_or_images.what_to_look_at: Geek+ RMS directly controls AGVs, handles movement, determines routing, and separates RMS responsibilities from onboard AGV sensing.

source_refs.quote_or_summary: Transcript says the office software, described as WCS, assigns robot tasks that go into a queue in RMS, and RMS executes them one by one by sending commands to the AGV.

missing_details: The source does not provide UI navigation steps.
The source does not provide queue names or queue configuration details.
The source does not provide command syntax, API details, or interface payloads for this flow.
The source provides a high-level overview only, not a troubleshooting decision tree.
